content.text: A repeat incident is one that shares the root cause of a prior incident whose postmortem action items were not completed, or were completed but did not actually close the systemic gap. Repeat incidents are the clearest signal that the postmortem process is not producing effective prevention.

Recurring incident prevention tracking closes the gap between 'we wrote the action items' and 'the failure cannot recur'. It requires three distinct capabilities: detecting when an incident matches a prior pattern, verifying that prevention measures were effective, and responding structurally when a prevention measure fails.

attrs.alt: Recurring incident detection and prevention pipeline: new incident declared, signature extracted, library match check — if match found, REPEAT FLAG raised and IC notified with prior action item re-opened; if no match, indexed as a new pattern. Prevention effectiveness loop at the bottom reviews whether closed action items actually prevented recurrence.

content.text: An incident signature is a compact representation of an incident's failure mode that can be compared against a library of prior incidents. Signatures are extracted from the postmortem metadata at declaration time, before the full root cause analysis is complete.
Primary system where the failure originated
Type of failure at the system level

At declaration time, the new incident's signature is compared against the library of closed postmortems. A match on 3 of 4 dimensions triggers a REPEAT flag. The IC is notified immediately, and the prior postmortem is linked in the new incident channel.

When a REPEAT flag fires, the IC initiates a brief repeat-incident check alongside the normal incident response. This does not slow down the mitigation work — it runs in parallel and feeds the postmortem.
IC reads the prior postmortem summary (linked in the channel) and confirms whether the current failure mode matches
IC checks whether the prior action items were closed — the incident bot reports this automatically
If action items were closed: the prevention measure may have failed or may not have addressed the actual root cause
If action items were not closed: the failure recurred because the prevention work was not done — this becomes a critical postmortem finding
IC documents the repeat check finding in the incident timeline: 'Repeat flag confirmed — prior action items {status}'

If the REPEAT flag fires but the IC determines the current failure mode is actually different from the prior incident, they clear the flag with a written explanation. False positives in the signature matcher are normal — the IC's judgement is the final gate.
A repeat incident postmortem has two additional mandatory sections beyond the standard template.
Link to the prior postmortem; explain why the prior action items did not prevent this recurrence
For each prior action item: was it completed? Did it address the actual failure mode? Why did it fail to prevent recurrence?

Closing a prevention action item means the implementation is done, not that the prevention works. Prevention effectiveness verification is the separate step of confirming that the deployed change actually eliminates or materially reduces the risk of the failure mode.
Fire the alert in a staging or chaos environment using the same conditions as the original incident
Alert fired, time-to-alert recorded, PagerDuty confirmed
Inject the incident-level load or failure condition in staging; confirm alert fires before customer impact
Deploy to staging; replay the original failure-triggering event; confirm failure does not occur
Apply the configuration in staging; verify the prior failure mode is blocked
Another engineer follows the runbook cold; confirm they can execute without asking for clarification
The updated process is applied to the next 3 similar events; confirm no deviations

A prevention measure failure occurs when an action item was closed (implementation was done) but the failure recurred. This is distinct from an action item being left open — it means the team believed they had fixed the issue but had not.
Confirm the implementation was actually deployed — check deployment logs for the specific change
Confirm the implementation was correct — compare the deployed configuration or code with the intended change
Assess whether the failure mode changed — did the failure recur via the same mechanism, or via a new path that the prevention did not cover?
Assess whether the scope was correct — did the prevention address the specific component that failed, or a different component?
Document the prevention failure analysis in the new postmortem as the 'prior action item failure' section
Action item was closed as done but change never reached production
Add deployment verification to the action item completion criterion process
Change was deployed but contained a bug or misconfiguration
Fix and redeploy; add automated test to catch the specific bug
Prevention addressed one instance of the failure mode but not all
Broader prevention item with wider scope; possibly involves multiple teams
System changed after the prevention was deployed, creating a new path to the same symptom
New action item targeting the evolved system; existing prevention is still valid
Action item was vague; implementation was technically done but did not close the risk
Rewrite the completion criterion more specifically; re-implement
The repeat failure rate is the single most important metric for postmortem process effectiveness. It measures whether the learning loop is closing risks, not just documenting them.

def compute_repeat_failure_metrics(
    postmortems: list[dict],
    window_days: int = 90,
) -> dict:
    from datetime import date, timedelta
    cutoff = date.today() - timedelta(days=window_days)
    recent = [pm for pm in postmortems if pm["date"] >= str(cutoff)]
    total = len(recent)
    repeats = [pm for pm in recent if pm.get("is_repeat", False)]
    # Prevention failures: items closed but failure recurred
    prevention_failures = [
        pm for pm in recent
        if pm.get("prior_action_items_all_closed", False) and pm.get("is_repeat", False)
    ]
    # Unclosed action item repeats: items were open when the failure recurred
    unclosed_repeats = [
        pm for pm in recent
        if not pm.get("prior_action_items_all_closed", True) and pm.get("is_repeat", False)
    ]
    return {
        "total_incidents": total,
        "repeat_count": len(repeats),
        "repeat_rate": len(repeats) / total if total else 0,
        "prevention_failure_count": len(prevention_failures),
        "unclosed_action_repeat_count": len(unclosed_repeats),
        "trend": "improving" if compute_trend(postmortems, "repeat_rate") < 0 else "worsening",
    }
Audit of action item completion criterion quality
Immediate repeat analysis; prior prevention measure invalid
Beyond responding to repeats when they occur, teams can reduce repeat incident probability through proactive measures based on their incident signature library.

Quarterly, engineers review the incident signature library for clusters of similar signatures. Clusters indicate that a systemic condition keeps triggering the same failure mode across multiple incidents that each look slightly different. A signature cluster is a candidate for a broader architectural change.

A game day exercise deliberately triggers a controlled version of a known failure mode to verify that prevention and detection measures work as intended. Game days should specifically target the top-3 recurring signature clusters in the postmortem library.
Schedule a game day within 60 days of any incident that was tagged as a repeat failure
The game day scenario is designed from the incident signature, not from scratch
Success criterion: alert fires within SLA, mitigation runbook completes without improvisation
Failure finding from the game day becomes a new action item — same process as a postmortem action item

Recurring incident prevention tracking consists of three interconnected processes: signature-based repeat detection at declaration time, prevention effectiveness verification at action item closure, and prevention failure analysis when a repeat occurs despite closed action items. Together they close the gap between writing action items and actually reducing recurrence probability.
Incident signatures: affected system + failure category + symptom pattern + trigger type — match on 3 of 4 triggers REPEAT flag
REPEAT flag initiates a parallel check during incident response; does not slow down mitigation
Prevention effectiveness requires independent verification — someone other than the owner must confirm the measure works
Prevention failure types: not deployed, incorrect implementation, scope too narrow, failure mode evolved, underspecified criterion
Target: repeat failure rate < 10%, prevention failure rate < 5%, mean time between repeats > 180 days
Signature cluster analysis identifies systemic conditions producing multiple similar incidents — candidates for architectural change
